political_offices.title: Select Man (Selectman)
political_offices.notes: Chosen at general town meeting Feb 4, 1634 along with Haynes, Bradstreet, and four others

political_offices.title: Representative
political_offices.notes: One of Representatives for Newtown (Cambridge), May 14, 1634

emigration.notes: 123 passengers including 50 children; all arrived in good health after 12-week passage. Fellow passengers included William Wadsworth, Tobie Willet (possible relation to Mary Willet who married Rev. Samuel Hooker), James Olmstedd, and others of Hooker's Company.

hartford_land.notes: Nine parcels originally granted: dwelling house on east side of Main Street abutting Centinel Hill; seven acres east of Great River; five acres in North Meadow; forty-seven acres meadow/swamp in North Meadow; one acre in Little Meadow; thirty acres in Old Oxpasture; seventeen acres in Cow Pasture; eleven acres in Venturers Field; sixteen acres in Neck of Land. Also gained 25 additional parcels by acquisition.

will.bequests_summary: Dwelling house and lands to wife Dorothy for her natural life; then to son John. Wethersfield property to son Samuel. Grandchildren John Russell (£20), John Talcott, Samuel Talcott, Elizabeth Talcott (£5 each). £5 to Rev. Samuel Stone; £5 toward a Latin school at Hartford. Overseers: Richard Lord Sr. and son John.
will.gift_to_stone: Five pounds to 'my reverend and beloved teacher, Mr. Stone' — Rev. Samuel Stone, Hartford minister and colleague of Thomas Hooker

parents.discrepancy_note: One source gives parents as 'John Mott and Alice Harington'; Talcott 1876 gives 'Mark Mott and Frances Gutter' with Mark being son of Thomas and Alice (Mead) Mott. The Talcott 1876 reconstruction preferred as more detailed.
parents.mother_research_notes: Probably Frances Gutter (wife of Mark Mott). Frances died Braintree, buried Feb 23, 1615. Mark Mott died Braintree, buried Dec 14, 1637.
parents.father_research_notes: Probably Mark Mott, son of Thomas and Alice (Mead) Mott of Braintree, Essex. Talcott 1876 says 'supposed to have been the daughter of Mark, son of Thomas and Alice (Mead) Mott.'

sources.full: Mary (Talcott) Russell: only daughter of the Worshipful John & Dorothy (Mott) Talcott; m. 28 Jun 1649; died ~1650 soon after confinement (no death record survives).

narrative_blocks.header: The Worshipful John Talcott's only daughter
narrative_blocks.body: Mary was the eldest child and only daughter of the magistrate John Talcott of Hartford. Born in England, she came with the family in the great migration. She grew up in one of the colony's leading households.

narrative_blocks.header: She married the future Angel of Hadley
narrative_blocks.body: In June 1649 Mary wed the young minister John Russell at Hartford. She bore him a son before her early death around 1658. Her husband would later become famous as the protector of the regicides.

sources.full: Descendants of the Rev. John Russell of Cambridge, Wethersfield, and Hadley
Primary sketch of Rev. John Russell of Hadley: Harvard 1645, Wethersfield to Hadley, sheltering the regicides, 1693 estate inventory (incl. three enslaved people), Hopkins School fight.

notable.notable_blurb: Puritan minister; the Angel of Hadley

narrative_blocks.header: The minister who hid the king’s killers
narrative_blocks.body: For over a decade Russell secretly sheltered Edward Whalley and William Goffe — two of the judges who had condemned Charles I to death — beneath his Hadley parsonage while royal agents hunted them across the colonies. A single misstep meant death for him and his household. The historian George Sheldon later called him the “Guardian Angel of Hadley.”

narrative_blocks.header: An angel rallied Hadley, then vanished
narrative_blocks.body: During a King Philip’s War raid, an unknown gray-haired stranger appeared, took command of the panicked townspeople, drove off the attackers, and disappeared — leaving them convinced an angel had saved them. The deliverer was almost certainly the fugitive general Goffe, slipping from Russell’s house and back. The episode became New England’s enduring “Angel of Hadley” legend.

narrative_blocks.header: He led his flock out of Connecticut
narrative_blocks.body: When a bitter dispute over baptism and church membership split the Wethersfield congregation, Russell drew up a covenant and gathered the dissenting families. In 1659 they agreed to leave the jurisdiction of Connecticut for Massachusetts. They crossed the wilderness and founded the town of Hadley on a bend of the Connecticut River.

narrative_blocks.header: Bones found beneath the parsonage cellar
narrative_blocks.body: When Russell’s house was pulled down near the end of the eighteenth century, workers lifting a cellar slab uncovered large human remains. Local tradition held them to be one of the regicides, secretly buried in the minister’s own foundation. A tooth from the find was kept by a witness for decades afterward.

narrative_blocks.header: Three enslaved people in his estate
narrative_blocks.body: The inventory of Russell’s estate, taken at Hadley in January 1693, listed an enslaved man, woman, and child together valued at sixty pounds. They are recorded among the household goods, a stark line in the ledger of a celebrated minister.

cross_connections.display_label: led the dissenting party Russell joined toward Hadley

sources.full: Descendants of the Rev. John Russell of Cambridge, Wethersfield, and Hadley
John Russell (son of Mary Talcott): bapt. 23 Sep 1650 First Church Hartford; d. 20 Jan 1670 Hadley; named in John Talcott's 1659 will bequest.

parents.notes: Mother_id deliberately null — see mother_notes for dispute
parents.mother_research_notes: Per Russell descendants book (later, authoritative): Jonathan was most likely the son of Rebecca Newberry (second wife of Rev. John Russell), not Mary Talcott. Evidence: Russell was already married to Rebecca Newberry by autumn 1651 per Henry Wolcott's ledger; Jonathan born Sept 18, 1655 — four years into that second marriage. Talcott 1876 assumed Mary Talcott as mother but had no direct record. Russell book's evidence accepted here.

sources.full: Descendants of the Rev. John Russell of Cambridge, Wethersfield, and Hadley
Descendants of the Rev. John Russell of Cambridge, Wethersfield, and Hadley

cross_connections.display_label: was his maternal grandfather, founding patriarch of the Hartford Talcott line

narrative_blocks.header: He baptized 452 souls in one parish
narrative_blocks.body: Rev. Jonathan Russell led the Barnstable church for twenty-eight years, from 1683 until his death in 1711. Contemporaries remembered him as a pious and learned divine; during his pastorate 171 members joined the church and 452 were baptized.

narrative_blocks.header: His estate held a surgeon's instruments
narrative_blocks.body: When his estate was inventoried in 1711, it listed drugs and a set of surgeon's instruments alongside his books. Like many frontier ministers of his era, he seems to have tended his congregation's bodies as well as their souls.

political_offices.title: Deputy / Assistant Magistrate
political_offices.notes: Elected before Connecticut joined New Haven Colony

political_offices.title: Treasurer
political_offices.notes: Succeeded his father May 17, 1660; held office until 1676

political_offices.title: Major, then Lieut.-Colonel
political_offices.notes: Resigned Treasurer and took command of Connecticut army at outbreak of King Philip's War

notable.notable_blurb: Colonial military commander, King Philip's War

narrative_blocks.header: He was named guardian of the charter
narrative_blocks.body: When Connecticut's royal charter arrived from Charles the Second in 1662, the freemen chose Talcott, with Samuel Wyllys and Lieutenant John Allyn, as the committee sworn to keep it in their custody. A quarter-century later he would help spirit that same charter away from Andros.

narrative_blocks.header: He took the charter as the candles died
narrative_blocks.body: By Roger Wolcott's 1764 account, when the candles were snuffed in the Hartford meeting-house in 1687, Talcott and Nathaniel Stanley lifted the two colonial charters from the table before Andros could seize them. Wolcott's telling names no oak; the tree entered the tradition later, and the duplicate passed to his son-in-law Joseph Wadsworth.

narrative_blocks.header: Connecticut's victorious commander in King Philip's War
narrative_blocks.body: Talcott led Connecticut's forces through King Philip's War in 1675 and 1676, winning a string of engagements that earned him promotion to lieutenant-colonel. His campaigns ranged from the river towns down into the Narragansett country.

narrative_blocks.header: He kept the colony's purse for sixteen years
narrative_blocks.body: Talcott served as treasurer of Connecticut Colony from 1660 to 1676, taking the office directly from his father before him. The family's account books became a running ledger of early Hartford.

narrative_blocks.header: He died intestate, his estate worth thousands
narrative_blocks.body: Talcott died in 1688 without a will, leaving an estate appraised at more than two thousand pounds, a substantial colonial fortune. It passed by primogeniture to his eldest surviving son, the future governor Joseph Talcott.

battles: Multiple engagements with Wampanoag and allied tribes
military_service.notes: Led Connecticut standing army with 200 Mohegan and Pequot Indian allies. 'In the various battles with the Indians in which he was engaged, he was always victorious, and obtained great renown as an Indian fighter.' Promoted from Major to Lieut.-Colonel during the war.

parents.notes: Her father John Wakeman was Colony Treasurer — the same office her husband John Talcott Jr. would later hold
parents.father_research_notes: John Wakeman, Treasurer of the Colony of Connecticut

sources.full: Descendants of Joseph and Elizabeth (Talcott) Wadsworth — later publication

cross_connections.display_label: Her son married his daughter, first Hooker-Talcott merger.

political_offices.title: Governor
political_offices.notes: First Governor of Connecticut born within its limits. Longest-serving after Gov. Winthrop. Died in office Oct 11, 1741.

cross_connections.display_label: Son-in-law signed his estate — 'Nathaniel Hooker and Eunice Hooker'
His granddaughter Esther married Rev. Noadiah Russell in 1758
was the Deputy Governor whose death opened his path

notable.notable_blurb: First Connecticut governor born within its limits

narrative_blocks.header: First Connecticut governor born within the colony
narrative_blocks.body: Born in Hartford on November 16, 1669, Joseph Talcott was the first Governor of Connecticut born within the colony rather than in England. He served from 1724 until his death on October 11, 1741 — 17 years in office, longer than any predecessor except Governor Winthrop and any successor until the modern era. His funeral expenses of £69 12s. 6d. were paid from the Colonial Treasury.

narrative_blocks.header: He presided in the upper house the afternoon
narrative_blocks.body: When his wife Eunice died suddenly about noon on Thursday, May 25, 1738, the Deputy Governor was absent at Norwich. Governor Talcott did not let his private grief interfere with his official duty — he returned and presided in the upper house that same afternoon. The General Assembly appointed a committee to draw an address of condolence to the Governor.

narrative_blocks.header: His estate was signed by 'Nathaniel Hooker
narrative_blocks.body: At the Court of Probate, Hartford, on December 25, 1742, the distribution of Governor Talcott's estate was signed by all children as co-heirs. Among the signatures: 'Nathaniel Hooker and Eunice Hooker' — his daughter Eunice Talcott and her husband Nathaniel Hooker 2d. The Talcott-Hooker convergence was entered into the public record.

narrative_blocks.header: Three of his children each found a separate
narrative_blocks.body: His daughter Eunice married Nathaniel Hooker 2d — direct Hooker surname. His son Matthew married Mary Russell, a Hooker descendant through the Pierpont branch. His daughter Abigail married Rev. Daniel Wadsworth; their son Jeremiah later married Mary's own sister Mehitable Russell, also a Hooker descendant.

narrative_blocks.header: Petitioned for his father's estate by primogeniture
narrative_blocks.body: Joseph Talcott was the fourth son of Lt.-Col. John Talcott Jr. but the eldest to survive his father. In 1691, at age 21, he petitioned the General Court for his father's estate by right of primogeniture — an assertive legal claim for colonial Connecticut. He was first mentioned in public life in 1711 as a committee member to lay out the town of Coventry.

cross_connections.display_label: Rachel's husband Gershom Buckley was the son of Peter Buckley.

political_offices.notes: From 1683 except during Andros' administration, was Assistant until his death

will.summary: Will of Captain Samuel Talcott, Wethersfield CT. Bequeathed to sons: Samuel (home lot south, great meadow parcels, Westfield lot); John (home lot north, 5 acres great meadow, 12 acres Westfield); Elizur (Westfield brook land, 6 acres meadow, 50-acre lot near Farmington line); Joseph (30 acres Westfield); Benjamin (north half, east side Connecticut River, undivided Indian lands, south half 200-acre court grant); Nathaniel (south half Nawburk lot, undivided east-river land, north half 200-acre grant, one cow). Daughters: Hannah Chester £90; Rachel £80. Wife Mary: £10/yr, use of lower room, garden, one cow during widowhood. Executor: son Samuel Talcott. Overseers: Capt. Robert Wells, Ensign Samuel Butler.
will.note: Original will text reproduced in Talcott Pedigree (1876) pp.97-102.

T00036.notable_legacy: "From these two sons, John and Samuel, are descended all of the name of TALCOTT in America." — Talcott Pedigree (1876). All living Talcotts in America descend from Capt. Samuel and/or his brother John (T00035).

narrative_blocks.header: Harvard College 1658
narrative_blocks.body: Harvard College 1658 — the scholar of the Talcott family; his father bequeathed all his books to Samuel (except the 'Martyr Book' given to John)

narrative_blocks.body: One of the original proprietors of the town of Glastonbury, CT — owned the lot purchased by his father John in 1643 of Samuel Sherman and Richard Gildersleeve, 44 rods wide running three miles to the wilderness; still owned by descendants at time of writing

bio.notes: Surname unknown. Second wife of Capt. Samuel Talcott.

narrative_blocks.header: John died young
narrative_blocks.body: John died young; the year was not recorded.

burial.plot_notes: Green Cemetery, Glastonbury, Hartford County, Connecticut.

will.summary: Will of Lt. Benjamin Talcott, Glastonbury CT. Wife: £80 + use of parlor, cellar, garret, ovens, gardens, one-third of orchard and land west of highway to great river — during widowhood only. Son Benjamin: lands in Bolton + 124 acres Hebron (bought of Joseph and John Phelps). Son John: 300 acres Hebron (west of Jacob Root), 50 acres Hebron (north of Richard Curtis), one yoke of steers, £10 moveable, + £15 from Elizur. Son Samuel: south half of home lot, 153 acres beyond Black Ledge River (Glastonbury/Hebron border), half undivided Glastonbury lands, smith-shop with all smithery tools, longest gun, half team. Son Elizur: north half of home lot with all buildings (except smith-shop), 60 acres Ash Swamp Glastonbury, 96 acres near bare hill Glastonbury, other half undivided lands, newest gun, half team — he to pay John £15. Daughter Sarah: £100. Daughter Hannah: £100 + decent suit of clothes or £10. Daughter Mehitabel: £100 + decent suit of clothes or £10. Executors: son-in-law Jonathan Hale and son Benjamin Talcott. Witnesses: Samuel Smith Jr., Nathaniel Talcott, Thomas Wells. Estate inventory: £2563 15s. 8d.
will.note: Will text reproduced in Talcott Pedigree (1876) pp.106-109.

cross_connections.display_label: Glastonbury homestead passed intact to him 175 years later.

narrative_blocks.header: His house bore bullet marks until 1854
narrative_blocks.body: He built a fortified wooden house on Main Street, Glastonbury in 1699 — a refuge from Indian attacks for the surrounding community. The house bore bullet marks until it was taken down soon after 1854.

narrative_blocks.header: Wife Sarah died in childbed at thirty-four
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah Hollister, granddaughter of original 1636 Hartford settler John Hollister, married him in 1699 and bore him eight children. She died in childbirth at the homestead October 15, 1715.

narrative_blocks.header: Sons signed Bolton's 1731 founding patent
narrative_blocks.body: Two of his sons Benjamin and Nathaniel were listed as Bolton Proprietors in the 1731 patent, signed by their cousin Governor Joseph Talcott. A third son Elizur later chaired Glastonbury's denunciation of the Boston Port Bill.

narrative_blocks.header: Two Wright cousins married two Talcott siblings
narrative_blocks.body: His son Elizur married Ruth Wright; his daughter Mehitabel married Ruth's first cousin Hezekiah Wright. Two Wright cousins thus married two Talcott siblings, weaving the families together.

narrative_blocks.header: Farm in family from 1643 through 1876
narrative_blocks.body: His grandfather John Talcott had bought the Glastonbury farm from Samuel Sherman and Richard Gildersleeve in 1643. The land was still owned by descendant Jared G. Talcott in 1876.

bio.bio_blurb: Twice-married woman the records nearly lost

narrative_blocks.header: Husband lost at sea two years into marriage
narrative_blocks.body: Rachel Talcott married Peter Bulkeley, a Wethersfield mariner, March 21, 1700. He was lost at sea in a storm about November 22, 1701, at age 41 — leaving no children. Probate found 'parts of a broken sloop at Block Island' and 'money due at Delaware.' She remarried Lt. Henry Wolcott of Windsor about 1704.

narrative_blocks.header: The sea took her husband, not her
narrative_blocks.body: Her first husband was lost at sea in 1701, and for years the records assumed she had died with him. In fact she lived on, married a second time, and raised three children before her death a quarter-century later.

burialCemetery.name: Ancient Burying Ground
burialCemetery.notes: Adjacent to Center Church. Earliest surviving identifiable gravestone dates to 1648. Resting place of many Hartford founders including Rev. Thomas Hooker (exact location unknown).
